court: Supreme Court of Judicature, Court of Appeal (Criminal Division)
judge: Lord Justice Dyson, Mr Justice Crane, His Honour Judge Radford
case_summary:
This case arises from an appeal by the Crown under section 58 of the Criminal Justice Act 2003 against a ruling of 'no case to answer' by His Honour Judge Elwen at Southwark Crown Court. The prosecution's case centred on a money service business called Kaz Money Exchange ('KME'), owned by SK and assisted by his son IK. Between April and December 2003, KME deposited approximately £60 million into its sterling account, but its daily reconciliation records showed a discrepancy of £5.9 million. To conceal this, SK and IK allegedly fabricated over 2,500 false customer receipts and customer summaries referencing a fictitious Pakistan entity ('Wall Street Lahore'). 

Separately, MR, who ran a legitimate grocery store called 'Ambrosia', systematically cheated the Revenue of tax and VAT by under-reporting sales from one of his two tills. MR attempted to launder £200,000 of these undeclared takings to Pakistan via KME; a portion was intercepted hidden under fruit in a box. Under Count 12, SK and MR were charged with money laundering under section 328(1) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 ('POCA 2002') for arranging to launder these undeclared takings. 

The trial judge withdrew Count 12 from the jury, holding that under R v Gabriel [2006] EWCA Crim 229, profits from legitimate trading cannot constitute 'criminal property' simply because they are undeclared. He then withdrew Counts 1 and 2 against SK and IK, on the basis that the £5.9 million discrepancy could also have been undeclared legitimate profits. 

The Court of Appeal allowed the Crown's appeal. Lord Justice Dyson analysed the statutory definitions of POCA 2002, noting that under section 340(6), a person who cheats the Revenue obtains a 'pecuniary advantage' equal to the unpaid tax, which constitutes a 'benefit' under section 340(5). Legitimate business takings 'represent' that benefit 'in part' under section 340(3)(a) because they incorporate the tax of which the Revenue has been cheated. The Court distinguished Gabriel, clarifying that while legitimate profits do not automatically become criminal property merely by non-disclosure, they do become criminal property once a tax cheat (the predicate offence) is committed. The Court also confirmed that a jury can properly infer the criminal provenance of unexplained funds (such as the £5.9 million discrepancy) from a defendant's dishonest conduct and wholesale falsification of records, without the Crown having to prove a specific predicate offence. Consequently, the terminating ruling in respect of IK on counts 1 and 2 was set aside.
aml_status_reasoning: This is an appeal by the prosecution under section 58 of the Criminal Justice Act 2003 against a terminating ruling of 'no case to answer' made by the trial judge. The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal in relation to counts 1 and 2 against the respondent IK, meaning the money laundering charges against him are reinstated and he must face trial; no final verdict has yet been reached for IK on these counts.

s328.title: Arrangements
s328.full: Section 328 of POCA 2002 provides that a person commits an offence if they enter into or become concerned in an arrangement which they know or suspect facilitates (by whatever means) the acquisition, retention, use, or control of criminal property by or on behalf of another person. This section is designed to catch professional intermediaries and facilitators of money laundering.
elements: Entering into or becoming concerned in an arrangement
Arrangement facilitates the acquisition, retention, use, or control of criminal property
Facilitation is by or on behalf of another person
Defendant knows or suspects the property is criminal property
s328.notes: Maximum penalty on indictment is 14 years' imprisonment. Often used against professional enablers (solicitors, accountants, bankers).
s328.confidence_reasoning: Directly quoted in the provided judgment snippet and is a core, highly familiar provision of POCA Part 7.

s328(1).title: Arrangements Offence
s328(1).full: Section 328(1) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 makes it an offence to enter into or become concerned in an arrangement which a person knows or suspects facilitates (by whatever means) the acquisition, retention, use or control of criminal property by or on behalf of another person.
elements: Entering into or becoming concerned in an arrangement
The arrangement facilitates the acquisition, retention, use, or control of criminal property
The facilitation is by or on behalf of another person
The defendant knows or suspects that the property is criminal property
s328(1).notes: This is one of the three principal money laundering offences in the UK. It carries a maximum penalty of 14 years' imprisonment on indictment. The mental element requires subjective knowledge or suspicion.
s328(1).confidence_reasoning: The provided judgment snippet explicitly references counts under section 328(1) of POCA and details the particulars of the offence, which matches the precise statutory wording of the arrangements offence.

s329(1).title: Acquisition, use and possession of criminal property
s329(1).full: Section 329(1) of POCA creates the offence of acquiring, using, or having possession of criminal property. It is one of the three principal money laundering offences in Part 7 of the Act.
elements: Offence of acquiring criminal property
Offence of using criminal property
Offence of having possession of criminal property
s329(1).notes: The prosecution must prove that the property was criminal property and that the defendant knew or suspected it represented the proceeds of crime.
s329(1).confidence_reasoning: The judgment snippet quotes section 329(1) verbatim, listing the elements of acquiring, using, and possessing criminal property.

s340.title: Interpretation
s340.full: Section 340 provides the key definitions for the money laundering provisions in Part 7 of POCA 2002. It defines 'criminal conduct' as conduct constituting an offence in any part of the UK (or which would do so if it occurred there) and 'criminal property' as property representing a person's benefit from criminal conduct, provided the offender knows or suspects it constitutes such a benefit.
elements: Definition of criminal conduct (s340(2))
Definition of criminal property (s340(3))
Definition of money laundering (s340(11))
Requirement of knowledge or suspicion for property to be 'criminal property'
s340.notes: This is the foundational interpretation section for all Part 7 offences. The definition of 'money laundering' includes attempts, conspiracies, and aiding/abetting of s327-329 offences.
s340.confidence_reasoning: The judgment snippet explicitly quotes s340(11) and s340(2)-(3), confirming the exact statutory definitions and structure.

s340(2).title: Definition of Criminal Conduct
s340(2).full: Section 340(2) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 defines 'criminal conduct' for the purposes of Part 7. It constitutes conduct which constitutes an offence in any part of the United Kingdom, or would constitute an offence in any part of the United Kingdom if it occurred there.
elements: Conduct constituting an offence in any part of the United Kingdom
Conduct occurring abroad that would constitute an offence if it had occurred in the United Kingdom
s340(2).notes: This subsection establishes the dual criminality principle for money laundering offences under Part 7, ensuring that extraterritorial conduct can generate 'criminal property' if it would be illegal in the UK.
s340(2).confidence_reasoning: This is a well-established statutory definition in POCA 2002 Section 340, which serves as the interpretation section for all Part 7 money laundering offences.

s340(3).title: Interpretation: Definition of criminal property
s340(3).full: Section 340(3) defines 'criminal property' for the purposes of the money laundering provisions in Part 7. Property is criminal property if it constitutes a person's benefit from criminal conduct, or represents such a benefit (in whole or in part, and whether directly or indirectly), and the alleged offender knows or suspects that it constitutes or represents such a benefit.
elements: The property constitutes or represents a person's benefit from criminal conduct (in whole or in part, directly or indirectly)
The alleged offender knows or suspects that the property constitutes or represents such a benefit (the subjective mens rea element)
s340(3).notes: This definition is foundational to the principal money laundering offences under sections 327, 328, and 329, which all require the subject matter to be 'criminal property'. It combines an objective requirement (that the property is actually derived from criminal conduct) with a subjective requirement (that the defendant knows or suspects this to be the case).
s340(3).confidence_reasoning: The definition is explicitly quoted and explained in the provided judgment snippet, which perfectly aligns with the statutory text of section 340(3) of POCA 2002.

s340(5).title: Definition of Benefiting from Conduct
s340(5).full: Section 340(5) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 provides that a person benefits from conduct if he obtains property as a result of or in connection with the conduct. This is a key component in determining whether property constitutes a benefit from criminal conduct.
elements: A person obtaining property
The property is obtained as a result of or in connection with the conduct
s340(5).notes: This subsection defines the act of 'benefiting' which is a prerequisite for property to be classified as 'criminal property' under section 340(3).
s340(5).confidence_reasoning: The provided judgment snippet explicitly references section 340(5) in relation to whether the proceeds of cheating the Revenue can amount to 'criminal property', and the statutory text of s340(5) is precisely recalled.

s340(6).title: Definition of Benefit
s340(6).full: Section 340(6) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002 states that if a person benefits from conduct, his benefit is the property obtained as a result of or in connection with the conduct. This subsection quantifies and identifies the 'benefit' itself as the specific property obtained.
elements: A person benefiting from conduct
The benefit is identified as the property obtained as a result of or in connection with that conduct
s340(6).notes: This works in tandem with section 340(5) to define the exact property that constitutes the 'benefit' for the purposes of identifying criminal property.
s340(6).confidence_reasoning: This is the exact statutory definition of 'benefit' under the interpretation provisions of Section 340 of POCA 2002.

poca_analysis:
The Court of Appeal's judgment provides a critical, authoritative analysis of the statutory mechanics under Part 7 of POCA 2002, specifically concerning the interaction of sections 328, 340(3), 340(5), and 340(6). 

Section 328(1) makes it an offence to enter into or become concerned in an arrangement facilitating the acquisition, retention, use, or control of criminal property. This section was engaged under Counts 1 and 2 in relation to the £5.9 million discrepancy in KME's accounts, and under Count 12 in relation to MR's £200,000 cash. 

To satisfy the definition of 'criminal property' under section 340(3), the property must constitute or represent a benefit from criminal conduct, and the offender must know or suspect this. The respondent argued that because MR's grocery business 'Ambrosia' was a legitimate trade, the cash takings could not be 'criminal property'. The Court of Appeal rejected this, using section 340(6), which deems that a person who obtains a 'pecuniary advantage' (such as unpaid tax/VAT through cheating the Revenue) is taken to have obtained a sum of money equal to that advantage. This sum is a 'benefit' under section 340(5). Consequently, the physical business takings (the undeclared cash) 'represent' that benefit 'in part' under section 340(3)(a). Thus, when a tax cheat (the predicate offence under s340(2)) is committed, the tax-evaded portion of the legitimate turnover becomes 'criminal property' for the purposes of the s328 laundering offence. This successfully links the civil/administrative tax evasion context directly to the criminal money laundering regime.
precedent_value: This case establishes the landmark legal precedent that the proceeds of tax evasion (such as cheating the Revenue of income tax and VAT) are capable of constituting 'criminal property' under POCA 2002, even if the business or trade generating those profits is entirely legitimate. It clarifies that a tax cheat creates a pecuniary advantage under section 340(6), which is represented 'in part' by the undeclared cash takings. It also limits the scope of R v Gabriel [2006] EWCA Crim 229, confirming that Gabriel only applies where no predicate tax cheat has been alleged or proved. Furthermore, the case is a leading authority for the proposition that the prosecution can prove the criminal origin of property via jury inference based on circumstantial evidence (such as records-falsification, discrepancies, and lies) without proving a specific predicate offence.
key_findings: A person who cheats the Revenue obtains a pecuniary advantage as a result of criminal conduct within the meaning of section 340(6) of the Proceeds of Crime Act 2002.

Under section 340(6) of POCA 2002, obtaining a pecuniary advantage is deemed equivalent to obtaining a sum of money equal to the value of that advantage, which constitutes a citable 'benefit' from criminal conduct under section 340(5).

Takings from a legitimate trade can represent a benefit from criminal conduct 'in part' under section 340(3)(a) of POCA 2002 once a tax fraud or cheat (the predicate crime) has been committed, because those takings incorporate the unpaid tax and VAT of which the Revenue has been cheated.

The decision in R v Gabriel [2006] EWCA Crim 229 does not establish that legitimate trading profits can never be criminal property; it holds only that such profits do not become criminal property per se simply by a failure to declare them, in the absence of a proven or alleged predicate tax fraud/cheat.

In a prosecution for money laundering under section 328 of POCA 2002, the Crown is not required to identify the specific predicate offence or the precise origin of the laundered funds; the jury may properly infer a criminal source from circumstantial evidence, including a massive accounting discrepancy and the systematic fabrication of records.

defendants.name: I K
defendants.verdict_reasoning: The trial judge's terminating ruling of 'no case to answer' was appealed by the prosecution under s58 of the Criminal Justice Act 2003. The Court of Appeal allowed the appeal, setting aside the acquittal and reinstating the money laundering charges under counts 1 and 2 against IK to proceed to trial.
defendants.poca_section_reasoning: IK's conduct engaged section 328(1) because he was allegedly concerned in an arrangement (the KME transaction book-shuffling and record falsification) which he knew or suspected facilitated the retention, use, or control of £5.9 million of criminal property (the unaccounted sterling deposits) by or on behalf of unidentified third parties.
key_facts: Assisted his father SK in operating the money service business Kaz Money Exchange (KME) on Green Street, London E13.
Indicted on Counts 1 and 2 for money laundering under section 328(1) of POCA 2002.
Linked to the concealment of a £5.9 million accounting discrepancy arising from £60 million deposited in KME's sterling account between April and December 2003.
Following the Court of Appeal's judgment on 8 March 2007, his terminating ruling of 'no case to answer' was reversed, and he was the sole remaining defendant ordered to face trial on counts 1 and 2.

sic_codes.reasoning: IK's primary work consisted of operating and managing cash deposits and ledger accounts for the KME money remittance business.

sic_codes.description: Activities auxiliary to financial intermediation n.e.c.
sic_codes.reasoning: IK engaged in auxiliary financial administration by preparing daily reconciliation sheets and generating receipts for international transfers.

defendants.sic_overall_reasoning: IK was an individual operator who assisted his father, SK, in running the money service and transfer business, Kaz Money Exchange (KME), located on Green Street, London. His professional activities are entirely tied to the financial remittance and MSB sector.

defendants.name: S K
defendants.verdict_reasoning: The trial judge wrongly accepted the submission of no case to answer on Count 12 (laundering MR's money) and subsequently on Counts 1 and 2, directing the jury to acquit SK. Due to procedural rules associated with the s58 CJA 2003 appeal, his directed acquittal stood and was not overturned on appeal.
defendants.poca_section_reasoning: SK was alleged to have operated KME as a laundering conduit for £5.9 million in unaccounted cash (Counts 1 & 2) and to have arranged to launder £200,000 of undeclared cash on behalf of MR (Count 12), knowing or suspecting it facilitated the control of criminal property.
key_facts: Owner and operator of Kaz Money Exchange (KME), which deposited approximately £60 million into its sterling bank account between 1 April and 6 December 2003.
Stopped by HMCE on 6 October 2003 regarding two men carrying £197,000 cash to KME, for which he provided demonstrably false receipts and business records to explain its origin.
Charged on Counts 1, 2, and 12 with money laundering under s328(1) of POCA, and Counts 3-7 with false accounting under s17(1)(a) of the Theft Act 1968.
Received a direct acquittal from the jury on the trial judge's direction following the erroneous 'no case to answer' ruling.

sic_codes.reasoning: SK owned and directed KME, which provided cash remittance and transmission services, a form of non-deposit financial intermediation.

sic_codes.description: Activities auxiliary to financial intermediation n.e.c.
sic_codes.reasoning: SK's role involved the physical management, security, and administrative sorting of large cash volumes deposited by clients.

defendants.sic_overall_reasoning: SK was the owner and principal operator of Kaz Money Exchange (KME), a registered money service business (MSB) that processed approximately £60 million in cash and bank deposits for international transfers to Pakistan.

defendants.name: M R
defendants.verdict_reasoning: The trial judge ruled there was no case to answer on Count 12, reasoning that the £200,000 cash comprised legitimate trading takings and could not constitute criminal property. Although the Court of Appeal ruled this legal logic was incorrect, MR's directed acquittal on Count 12 stood and was not reinstated, though his prosecution for Count 11 (the underlying tax cheat) remained unaffected.
defendants.poca_section_reasoning: MR engaged s328(1) by entering into an arrangement (using KME to transfer undeclared cash to Pakistan) which he knew or suspected facilitated the retention, use, or control of his own criminal property (undeclared takings representing tax/VAT of which the Revenue had been cheated).
key_facts: Operated 'Ambrosia', a two-unit grocery and greengrocery business located 100 meters from KME's head office.
Charged on Count 11 with the common law offence of cheating the Revenue by hiding takings from one of his business tills.
Seized £100,000 cash hidden under fruit in a box from two couriers at KME's door on 6 December 2003, and admitted to a prior transfer of £100,000 on 14 November 2003.
Indicted under Count 12 with money laundering under s328(1) of POCA for attempting to transfer the £200,000 in undeclared takings to Pakistan via KME.

sic_codes.description: Retail sale of fruit and vegetables in specialised stores
sic_codes.reasoning: MR operated a greengrocery business and hid his under-declared retail cash under a box of physical fruit.

sic_codes.description: Retail sale in non-specialised stores with food, beverages or tobacco predominating
sic_codes.reasoning: MR's business 'Ambrosia' operated as a two-unit grocery store retailing various foodstuffs and general merchandise to local consumers.

defendants.sic_overall_reasoning: MR was the sole proprietor of 'Ambrosia', a two-unit grocery and greengrocery business. His commercial activity was strictly retail trade, selling food products, fruit, and vegetables to the public.

defendants.name: Kaz Money Exchange
defendants.verdict_reasoning: KME was the corporate vehicle and platform used by SK and IK to facilitate the alleged money laundering transactions. It was not indicted or prosecuted as a separate corporate defendant in these appellate proceedings.
defendants.poca_section_reasoning: KME's corporate accounts and operations were the primary arrangement through which the £5.9 million of unexplained cash and MR's £200,000 in tax-evaded funds were integrated and transferred out of the UK jurisdiction.
key_facts: Operated as a money service and money transfer business located in Green Street, London E13.
Processed approximately £60 million in deposits through its sterling bank account between 1 April and 6 December 2003.
Showed a systematic £5.9 million deficit in its daily reconciliation records compared to its bank deposits.
Generated over 2,500 admittedly false customer receipts and falsified statements to a Pakistan entity called 'Wall Street Lahore' to mask the cash discrepancy.

sic_codes.reasoning: KME functioned as a financial remittance platform, receiving and depositing over £60 million in cash for transmission.

sic_codes.description: Activities auxiliary to financial intermediation n.e.c.
sic_codes.reasoning: The entity provided auxiliary transfer brokerage, money exchange, and payment processing services for retail clients.

defendants.sic_overall_reasoning: Kaz Money Exchange (KME) operated as a commercial money transfer and money service business (MSB), offering international funds transmission services, primarily processing sterling cash remittances destined for Pakistan.

defendants.name: Ambrosia
defendants.verdict_reasoning: Ambrosia was a sole proprietorship/trading name owned and operated by MR; it was not charged as a corporate entity in this indictment.
defendants.poca_section_reasoning: The business operations of Ambrosia generated the legitimate revenue which was partially converted into criminal property under s340 once MR systematically withheld tax/VAT, creating a pecuniary advantage under s340(6).
key_facts: A two-unit grocery and greengrocery business operated by MR in London.
Employed two cash tills but provided accountants with takings records from only one till, systematically under-declaring VAT and income tax.
Kept dual records, including secret 'memo books' capturing the true, higher business takings, which were withheld from tax authorities.
Generated the £200,000 in cash takings that MR attempted to transmit to Pakistan through KME's money remittance services.

sic_codes.description: Retail sale of fruit and vegetables in specialised stores
sic_codes.reasoning: Ambrosia is explicitly identified in the judgment as a greengrocery shop, operating specialised retail trading in fresh produce.

sic_codes.description: Retail sale in non-specialised stores with food, beverages or tobacco predominating
sic_codes.reasoning: The business functioned as a two-unit grocery retail shop, offering daily consumer goods and food items to the general public.

defendants.sic_overall_reasoning: Ambrosia was a physical, two-unit retail grocery and greengrocery shop in London that generated cash turnover through daily sales, a portion of which was systematically withheld from tax authorities.
